question: A Go service running as PID 1 inside a Docker container spawns child processes via os/exec.Command. Zombie processes accumulate under the container until PIDs exhaust and fork() returns EAGAIN. Running the same binary directly on the host (not as PID 1) never produces zombies. What kernel mechanism causes this difference, and what production fix properly prevents zombie accumulation in containers?
answer: PID 1 in a PID namespace inherits init semantics: orphaned processes reparent to it. Unlike host init (systemd), container PID 1 is an application binary that never calls waitpid(). Children from os/exec whose intermediate parent exits become orphans reparented to PID 1. Without a SIGCHLD handler calling waitpid() with WNOHANG in a loop, they persist as zombies until the PID table fills. Fix: Docker --init wraps with tini. Alternative: prctl(PR_SET_CHILD_SUBREAPER) with a drain loop in SIGCHLD.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[PID 1: Go binary in container] --> B[exec.Command sh -c sleep 1 &]
  B --> C[sh intermediate process]
  B --> D[sleep 1 grandchild]
  C -- sh exits --> E[sleep reparented to PID 1]
  E --> F{PID 1 calls waitpid in loop?}
  F -->|No - no SIGCHLD handler| G[ZOMBIE - PID slot consumed]
  F -->|Yes - tini or subreaper| H[Process table freed]
  G --> I[PID pool exhausts, fork returns EAGAIN]
